topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I stared blankly at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming into an indistinct blur. When Sarah mentioned the bonus structure for the new project, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I glanced over at Mark, who was animatedly discussing his recent promotion with a colleague. The casual ease with which he spoke of higher compensation felt like a physical blow. My hands started to tremble slightly, and I gripped my pen so tightly the plastic creaked. I just wanted the noise to stop, the pressure to lift. Every calculation seemed weighted down by the stark reality of the disparity, leaving me feeling heavy and utterly insignificant in the sterile cubicle farm.

[story 2]

I kept running my fingers over the worn edge of my desk, the grain rough beneath my fingertips. The afternoon sun, trying to pierce the dusty blinds, only managed to cast weak, sickly yellow stripes across the floor. Hearing the announcement about the salary adjustments echoed in my ears, sounding hollow and distant. I forced myself to look at the figures, the contrast between my own modest earnings and the figures Sarah had casually referenced felt like a chasm opening up beneath my feet. A tightness formed in my chest, a constricting sensation that made drawing a full breath feel like a chore. I kept my gaze fixed on the monitor, meticulously avoiding eye contact, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The silence in the breakroom was oppressive, broken only by the clatter of ceramic mugs. I watched as Sarah laughed, a bright, unrestrained sound that grated against my own internal quiet. When she brought up the topic of compensation, the air in the room seemed to thin out. I felt a sudden, sharp drop in my energy, a kind of numbness spreading through my limbs. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, my movements slow and uncoordinated. The world outside the breakroom seemed too loud, too demanding, and I felt utterly incapable of meeting the invisible expectations placed upon me.
